title: Isaiah 38
subtitle: Hezekiah’s Illness and Fifteen Added Years

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “I Will Add Fifteen Years to Your Life”

heb.paragraph: "I will add (yāsaf) fifteen years to your life" (v.5). Hezekiah falls deathly ill. Isaiah delivers God’s verdict: "Put your house in order, because you are going to die" (v.1). Hezekiah turns his face to the wall and weeps bitterly, reminding God of his faithfulness (vv.2–3). Before Isaiah leaves the middle court, God sends him back with a reversal: fifteen years added, Jerusalem delivered from Assyria (vv.5–6). The sign: the shadow on the sundial of Ahaz goes back ten steps (v.8). Time itself reverses to confirm the promise.

notes.note: "Put your house in order; you are going to die." Hezekiah turns to the wall and weeps. His prayer: "Remember how I have walked faithfully." The king who spread the letter now turns his face to the wall.
"I have heard your prayer and seen your tears. I will add fifteen years." God reverses a death-verdict because of prayer and tears. The verdict was real; the reversal is also real.
"The shadow went back the ten steps it had gone down." The sun-dial miracle: time reverses. The God who adds years also bends shadows.

refs.note: The parallel includes the sign of the shadow retreating ten steps, emphasizing the miraculous confirmation of healing.
Chronicles adds that Hezekiah's heart became proud after recovery, requiring later repentance — a complexity Isaiah omits.
The psalmist's plea that the dead cannot praise God anticipates Hezekiah's reasoning in his prayer.

hist.context: Hezekiah's illness occurred around 701 BC, during or shortly after the Assyrian crisis. The fifteen added years would place his death around 686 BC. The poultice of figs was standard ancient Near Eastern medicine for boils. The shadow retreating on Ahaz's sundial (or stairway) served as a visible sign confirming Isaiah's word — physical phenomena validating prophetic authority.

sections.header: Verses 9–22 — “The Living, the Living — They Praise You”

heb.paragraph: "The living, the living (ḥay) — they praise you, as I am doing today; parents tell their children about your faithfulness" (v.19). Hezekiah’s writing after his recovery: "I said, ‘In the prime of my life I must go through the gates of death’" (v.10). "Like a shepherd’s tent my house has been pulled down and taken from me" (v.12). "I cried like a swift or thrush; I moaned like a mourning dove" (v.14). The grief is raw and physical. Then the turn: "You restored me to health and let me live. Surely it was for my benefit that I suffered such anguish" (vv.16–17). The suffering was purposeful. "You have put all my sins behind your back" (v.17). Sins behind God’s back — where he cannot see them.

notes.note: "In the prime of my life. Like a weaver I have rolled up my life; he has cut me off from the loom." Three mortality metaphors: gates of death, shepherd’s tent pulled down, thread cut from the loom.
"You have put all my sins behind your back." Behind God’s back: the one place God chooses not to look. The imagery of deliberate divine forgetting.

"The living, the living — they praise you. Parents tell their children about your faithfulness." The purpose of survival: praise and testimony. The living praise; the dead cannot (Ps 88:10–12). Hezekiah’s extra years are for worship and witness.

refs.note: The psalm's questions about whether the dead praise God parallel Hezekiah's lament that Sheol cannot thank YHWH.
David's plea 'what profit is there in my blood?' uses the same logic as Hezekiah's appeal for healing.
Jonah's psalm from the fish's belly employs similar descent-and-deliverance imagery to Hezekiah's thanksgiving.

hist.context: Hezekiah's psalm (vv.10-20) is a thanksgiving for recovery, structured like individual psalms of lament that move from distress to praise. The imagery of Sheol and the Pit reflects standard Israelite afterlife concepts where the dead exist in shadowy half-life, unable to praise YHWH. The temple procession mentioned in v.20 indicates the psalm's liturgical use after recovery.

rows.label: The Living Praise — 38:9–22
rows.text: "THE LIVING praise you." Tent pulled down; thread cut. "You put my sins BEHIND YOUR BACK."

hebtext.note: "I will add (yāsaf) fifteen years to your life" (v.5). Hezekiah falls deathly ill. Isaiah delivers God’s verdict: "Put your house in order, because you are going to die" (v.1). Hezekiah turns his face to the wall and weeps bitterly, reminding God of his faithfulness (vv.2–3). Before Isaiah leaves the middle court, God sends him back with a reversal: fifteen years added, Jerusalem delivered from Assyria (vv.5–6). The sign: the shadow on the sundial of Ahaz goes back ten steps (v.8). Time itself reverses to confirm the promise.

"The living, the living (ḥay) — they praise you, as I am doing today; parents tell their children about your faithfulness" (v.19). Hezekiah’s writing after his recovery: "I said, ‘In the prime of my life I must go through the gates of death’" (v.10). "Like a shepherd’s tent my house has been pulled down and taken from me" (v.12). "I cried like a swift or thrush; I moaned like a mourning dove" (v.14). The grief is raw and physical. Then the turn: "You restored me to health and let me live. Surely it was for my benefit that I suffered such anguish" (vv.16–17). The suffering was purposeful. "You have put all my sins behind your back" (v.17). Sins behind God’s back — where he cannot see them.

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 9: death-verdict reversed, fifteen years added, sins behind God’s back. Worship: the living praise. Prayer changes God’s stated intention.

coaching.tip: Hezekiah's illness and recovery add fifteen years to his life (v. 5). But those fifteen years include showing Babylon's envoys everything in his treasury (ch. 39) — the act that seals Judah's exile. God's mercy doesn't guarantee wise use of the gift. The extended life becomes the occasion for the fatal mistake. Isaiah's narrative has the complexity of a novel, where answered prayer creates new problems.

'The living, the living — they praise you, as I am doing today' (v. 19). Hezekiah's psalm expresses an Old Testament theology of death: Sheol is silence, the grave cannot praise God. This is not yet resurrection hope — that comes in 26:19 and Daniel 12:2. Isaiah preserves the tension: even a faithful king has an incomplete understanding of death. The Bible's theology develops progressively, and Isaiah shows us a faith still reaching for answers.
